title: Research and Development in the Social Sciences and Humanities

alternatives.name: Taxable contract research provider
inclusion_criteria: Employer firm classified in NAICS 541720 whose establishments are subject to federal income tax.
Performs research and development in the social sciences or humanities for external clients, sponsors, or counterparties.
Estimated to fall in the $1 million to $10 million EBITDA band after normalizing owner compensation.
exclusion_criteria: Firm spanning both taxable and tax-exempt establishments, retained in the named residual.
Nonemployer business, government unit, university primarily classified in education, market-research firm classified in NAICS 541910, or management consultancy classified outside NAICS 541720.
alternatives.rationale: The base allocates the normalized 258-firm band count by the mutually exclusive taxable-only share inferred from the 2022 Economic Census national firm counts: 1,075 taxable firms plus 712 exempt firms versus 1,782 firms overall implies five mixed-status firms and 1,070 taxable-only firms; 258 x 1,070 / 1,782 rounds to 155. The low of 90 is the receipts-share allocation, 258 x $3.251 billion taxable receipts / $9.298 billion total receipts. The employment-share allocation is 123 and lies inside the range. These are population bridges rather than direct band-by-tax-status observations.

alternatives.name: Tax-exempt research institute
inclusion_criteria: Employer firm or organization classified in NAICS 541720 whose establishments are exempt from federal income tax.
Performs research and development in the social sciences or humanities as an independent nonprofit institute or analogous tax-exempt organization.
Estimated to fall in the $1 million to $10 million EBITDA-equivalent operating-surplus band after normalizing executive compensation.
exclusion_criteria: Firm with only establishments subject to federal income tax.
Firm spanning both taxable and tax-exempt establishments, retained in the named residual.
Government unit or university primarily classified in education rather than NAICS 541720.
alternatives.rationale: The base allocates the normalized band count by the 707 exempt-only firms inferred after removing the five mixed-status firms: 258 x 707 / 1,782 rounds to 102. The high of 168 is the receipts-share allocation, 258 x $6.047 billion exempt receipts / $9.298 billion total receipts. The employment-share allocation is 135 and lies inside the range. The alternative remains plausible as the largest band archetype because its supported range overlaps the selected archetype's range and exempt organizations have materially higher average receipts per establishment.

residual.name: Mixed-tax-status 541720 firms
residual.rationale: The national Economic Census reports 1,075 firms with taxable establishments and 712 with exempt establishments against 1,782 firms overall. The five-firm excess is retained as a mixed-status residual rather than assigned to either mutually exclusive archetype. Proportional allocation to the 258-firm band rounds to one; zero to five preserves uncertainty about whether any or all mixed-status firms fall in band.

sources.title: 2022 Economic Census: Professional, Scientific, and Technical Services Summary Statistics
2022 Economic Census: Industry by Products Statistics for the United States
2022 Statistics of U.S. Businesses Annual Data Tables by Establishment Industry
North American Industry Classification System, United States, 2022

target_archetype.selection_basis: The two named archetypes partition firms by federal income-tax status after retaining the five-firm mixed-status overlap as residual. Applying the 2022 national firm-count shares to the normalized 258-firm target band gives 155 taxable-only firms, 102 exempt-only firms, and one mixed-status firm. The largest base count therefore selects taxable-contract-research-provider. Because no source directly cross-tabulates the EBITDA band by tax status, receipts- and employment-share allocations were used as sensitivity bridges. The taxable range of 90-155 overlaps the exempt range of 102-168, so selection uncertainty is true even though the taxable base exceeds the exempt base by more than 10 percent.

value_basis.scope: Recurring cash operating costs of the selected taxable contract research provider after market-rate owner replacement compensation; includes employee compensation and recurring individual or firm contractors, and excludes taxes, interest, depreciation, amortization, and one-time transaction costs.

labor_contractor_cash_cost_share.rationale: Pre-frozen class-neutral bounded structure for a labor-intensive professional research provider. The wide range accommodates firm-level variation in respondent incentives, purchased data, travel, facilities, subcontracted fieldwork, and the unresolved reclassification into the overlapping tax-exempt alternative. It is not an empirical estimate.
caveats: No target-archetype source directly measures employee-plus-contractor cash cost as a share of recurring cash operating cost.
The normalized industry labor share is not substituted because it is a broader-NAICS revenue denominator and does not identify contractor cost or the selected taxable-only archetype.
The assumption basis forces overall readiness to UNPROVEN.

roles.title: Research directors and senior social scientists
roles.rationale: Mutually exclusive cash-cost bucket for research design, methodology, subject-matter leadership, scientific supervision, interpretation, and senior review.

roles.title: Research assistants, field staff, and data-collection staff
roles.rationale: Mutually exclusive cash-cost bucket for participant recruitment, interviewing, survey administration, document collection, fieldwork, coding, transcription, and routine research support.

roles.title: Statistical, data, and technical specialists
roles.rationale: Mutually exclusive cash-cost bucket for statistical analysis, data engineering, programming, database administration, visualization, and technical quality control.

roles.title: Project, client, proposal, and funding management
roles.rationale: Mutually exclusive cash-cost bucket for project management, client service, account management, proposals, contracting, business development, and sponsor or funder administration.

roles.title: Executive, finance, people, legal, IT, facilities, and administration
roles.rationale: Mutually exclusive cash-cost bucket for general management and internal support not assigned to research delivery, analytics, or project and commercial management.

value_basis.rationale: The role weights sum to 1.00 and freeze an exhaustive, non-overlapping allocation of labor and recurring contractor cash cost for the selected archetype. They are assumptions rather than sourced target-specific measurements; a broad industry occupation mix cannot safely establish taxable-provider weights, and the unresolved tax-status selection further prevents a deterministic dataset substitution.
caveats: Workers are assigned by primary function so that staff who perform multiple activities are counted once.
External contractors are assigned to the same functional bucket as equivalent employees.
Respondent incentives, purchased panels or data, travel, rent, software, insurance, and other nonlabor cash costs are outside role weights but inside the cash-cost-share denominator.
The role mix is frozen for reproducibility and must not be presented as observed market evidence.

source_hints.source: U.S. Census Bureau 2022 Economic Census EC2254BASIC and EC2200NAPCSINDPRD tables, with SUSB firm-size tables for the target-band denominator
source_hints.why: Reopen the national 541720 taxable and exempt firm, establishment, employment, receipts, and product rows; determine whether a direct band-by-tax-status cross-tab or a better firm-to-establishment bridge exists.

source_hints.area: Cash-cost structure and functional role weights
source_hints.source: Target-firm confidential information memoranda, quality-of-earnings reports, audited statements, IRS Form 990 functional-expense schedules for comparable exempt institutes, and BLS OEWS NAICS 541720 occupation tables
source_hints.why: Construct selected-archetype employee-plus-contractor cash cost and mutually exclusive functional weights without treating the broad industry occupation mix as target-specific evidence.

source_hints.source: USAspending.gov and SAM.gov award records, agency procurement files, public grant databases, and disclosed client-contract samples
source_hints.why: Measure customer concentration, contract type, recompete cadence, backlog, renewal behavior, price adjustment, deliverable acceptance, data rights, and termination provisions for comparable taxable research providers.

source_hints.source: Firm process maps, project time records, survey or fieldwork protocols, institutional review documentation, data-governance policies, and quality-management records
source_hints.why: Establish actual research-design, collection, analysis, review, reporting, privacy, and approval workflows and the resources required to change them.

source_hints.source: GF Data, DealStats, PitchBook, Capital IQ, transaction announcements, buyer filings, and deal materials for social-science research, evaluation, and government-services providers
source_hints.why: Identify completed control transactions, target size, buyer type, recurring-revenue mix, margins, consideration, and defensible entry and downside-exit multiple ranges for the selected archetype.

source_hints.source: Customer references, proposal and award histories, key-person and change-of-control clauses, employee rosters and tenure data, credential requirements, subcontractor agreements, and post-transition operating evidence
source_hints.why: Test whether client relationships, research credibility, contract eligibility, principal-investigator roles, data access, and delivery quality persist after ownership and leadership changes.

implementation: What are the selected archetype's end-to-end workflows for research design, instrument or protocol development, recruitment and collection, data preparation, analysis, quality review, reporting, and client acceptance?
Which systems of record, privacy or research-ethics controls, data-use agreements, approvals, specialized datasets, field operations, and subcontractors constrain process changes?
What firm-level evidence bounds the one-time cost, elapsed time, training, parallel operation, validation, and disruption associated with a material workflow or technology change?
commercial_retention: What shares of revenue are fixed-price, time-and-materials, cost-reimbursable, subscription or data access, grant-funded, and other arrangements?
What do contract cohorts show for renewal, recompete, scope expansion, price adjustment, cancellation, termination for convenience, and client concentration?
How do clients evaluate methodological credibility, independence, confidentiality, timeliness, and deliverable quality, and which of those requirements are contractual?
sale_availability: How many completed control sales of comparable taxable social-science or humanities research providers occurred in the most recent relevant period, and how many were in the $1 million to $10 million EBITDA band?
Which buyer types participated, what proportion of consideration was cash at close versus contingent or rolled equity, and what exclusions or add-backs affected reported EBITDA?
Which founder dependence, contract novation, small-business status, conflict, data-rights, or client-consent issues narrowed the transferable seller population?
valuation_robustness: What entry and exit multiple ranges are supported by completed transactions for this archetype after normalizing size, growth, margin, customer concentration, contract mix, and contingent consideration?
How do observed multiples vary between government-focused and commercial-client providers, prime and subcontractor revenue, project and recurring revenue, and founder-led and management-led firms?
What downside multiple is supported when backlog, recompete, key-person, client concentration, or margin-normalization risks are stressed?
operator_survival: Which client, sponsor, research-leadership, proposal, methodological, and institutional relationships depend personally on the seller or a small number of principals?
Do contracts, grants, registrations, certifications, data-use agreements, ethics approvals, or prime-contractor arrangements survive a change of control and replacement of the owner-operator?
What post-transition evidence supports retention of research staff, proposal win rates, client acceptance, delivery quality, and access to data and participant populations through year five?

special_notes: This record describes only the taxable-contract-research-provider archetype and must not be presented as a score or characterization of all NAICS 541720 firms.
Selection uncertainty is true because the supported taxable and tax-exempt band-count ranges overlap; overall readiness is therefore forced to UNPROVEN.
The normalized 258-firm target-band estimate has LOW quality and is not a direct census of firms with $1 million to $10 million EBITDA.

The base archetype allocation uses firm counts, while the range spans receipts- and employment-share sensitivity bridges because no published table directly crosses tax status with the target EBITDA band.
Federal income-tax status is an organizational-form boundary, not evidence about ownership quality, operating performance, saleability, or valuation.
The assumption-mode value basis has no empirical evidentiary weight, accepts no source claims, and independently forces readiness to UNPROVEN.
Tax-exempt operating surplus is used only to make the alternative's band concept comparable; it does not imply that an exempt organization is distributable or transferable like a taxable company.
